coredata.dc:title: Thermal-switchable sensor based on palladium-graphene composite and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Dan, Xu
Ruiyi, Li
Qinsheng, Wang
Yongqiang, Yang
Guangli, Wang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Improving dispersion of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheet still faces great challenges in fabrication of metal-graphene composite. The study reports one way for synthesis of palladium-graphene composite with histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) as linker and semiconductor. The resulting composite offers a three-dimensional architecture. Small Pd nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on graphene sheets. The intimate contacts of His-GQD with graphene and Pd nanoparticle create the Schottky heterojunction and leads to an improved catalytic activity. Thermal-switchable sensor based on the Pd-His-GQD-G and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) exhibits better sensitivity, selectivity and long-term stability for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol compared with the reported sensors. The differential pulse voltammetric current linearly increases with the increase of 4-nitrophenol in the range of 0.5–250 μM with detection limit is 0.10 μΜ (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method has been successfully applied in detection of 4-nitrophenol in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Noble metal nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dot
Graphene-based hybrid
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of temperature on 4-NP electrochemical behaviors at Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme (A), SEM image (B), TEM image (C), HRTEM image (D), EDS layered electron image (E), XRD pattern (F) and Raman spectrum (G) of the as-synthesized Pd-His-GQD-G.
The CV (A) and EIS (B) curves of bare GCE (a), PNIPAM/GCE (b) and Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE (c) in the PBS of 7.0 containing 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-.
The EIS curves (A) of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM in the PBS of 7 containing 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]4- at different temperatures of 16, 18, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36 and 38 ℃, and the Rct values at different temperatures.
Temperature-controlled electrochemical response of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE towards 4-NP.

DPV curves (A) of the proposed sensor in a PBS of 7.0 containing 0.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 55, 70, 80, 100, 115, 130, 140, 150, 175, 190, 200, 225 and 250 μM 4-NP and the relationship curve (B) of DPV peak current with the concentration of 4-NP.
Comparison of the proposed method for detection of 4-NP with other reported methods.
The results for detection of 4-NP in tap, lake and sea water samples (N = 5).

head.ce:title: Thermal-switchable sensor based on palladium-graphene composite and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu), Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute·Branch of Wuxi, Wuxi 214174, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu), Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute·Branch of Wuxi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu), Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute·Branch of Wuxi, Wuxi 214174, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College, Taizhou 225300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College, Taizhou 225300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improving dispersion of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheet still faces great challenges in fabrication of metal-graphene composite. The study reports one way for synthesis of palladium-graphene composite with histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) as linker and semiconductor. The resulting composite offers a three-dimensional architecture. Small Pd nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on graphene sheets. The intimate contacts of His-GQD with graphene and Pd nanoparticle create the Schottky heterojunction and leads to an improved catalytic activity. Thermal-switchable sensor based on the Pd-His-GQD-G and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) exhibits better sensitivity, selectivity and long-term stability for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol compared with the reported sensors. The differential pulse voltammetric current linearly increases with the increase of 4-nitrophenol in the range of 0.5–250 μM with detection limit is 0.10 μΜ (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method has been successfully applied in detection of 4-nitrophenol in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Palladium (Pd) has high heterogeneous catalysis, selectivity and electrocatalytic/photocatalytic activity and strong interaction with hydrogen molecules [1,2]. To date, Rd-based catalysts have been widely applied in organic synthesis, such as C − C cross-coupling reaction, redox reaction and tandem reaction [3–5], catalytic degradation [6], electrochemical sensor [7] and fuel cell [8]. Pd nanocrystal received more attention as sensing material for construction of electrochemical sensors due to its special catalytic activity. The studies demonstrated that Pd nanocrystal can selectively catalyze the electrode reaction and lead to improved sensitivity and selectivity. However, the performance improcement strongly depends on morphology, size and crystal structure of Pd nanocrystals [9].

Great effort was devoted to synthesis of Pd-based nanomaterials to further improve the catalytic activity. The first strategy is to reduce the size of Pd nanoparticles. A smaller size results in a better catalytic activity, because of its higher atomic utilization. Chatterjee et al. reported synthesis of Pd nanoparticles with a particle size of 2.1–7.1 nm for catalytic reduction of p-nitrophenol [10]. The result shows that the Pd nanoparticles of 2.1 nm have a much higher catalytic efficiency compared with the other Pd nanoparticles with a higher particle size. However, small nanoparticles always tend to agglomerate into large Pd nanoparticles during the catalysis, especially at high temperature. The second strategy is to adjust the morphology of Pd nanoparticles. Many investigations have revealed that the different crystal facet of Pd nanocrystal may show a different catalytic activity towards the redox reaction. Wang et al. reported synthesis of Pd nanoparticles composed of triangle, hexagon and decahedron for electrocatalytic oxidation of formic acid. Although all Pd nanoparticles display high electrocatalytic activity and stability for the oxidation of formic acid, the Pd nanoparticles with more exposed (111) facet gives a much better catalytic activity. This leads to an increased peak current for oxidation of formic acid [11]. Yang et al. reported one kind of cubic Pd nanocrystal for electrochemical determination of H2O2 [12]. The sensor based on cubic Pd has a high sensitivity of 488 μA mM−1 cmECSA -2 and a low detection limit of 0.6 μM. Such a good electrochemical performance should be mainly attributed to a higher catalytic activity of the {100} facet of Pd nanoparticles compared with other crystal facets. The (111) facet moderates the binding energy of *OH and accelerate the reduction of H2O2, leading to a higher sensitivity. The third strategy is to introduce carbon material such as graphene, carbon nitride, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanospheres and activated carbon was used for making Pd-based composite [13–17]. The results demonstrated that the introduction of carbon material can prevent aggregation of Pd nanoparticles and improve the catalytic activity and stability [18–19]. Among various carbon materials, graphene attracted more attention because of its large surface area, high electrical conductivity, good mechanical and thermal properties [20–21]. Li et al. made one Pd-graphene composite by reducing PdCl2 on graphene sheet with NaBH4. The resultant composite shows high dispersion and synergistic effect between Pd and graphene. This will greatly improve the catalytic activity. As a result, the sensor based on Pd-graphene composite exhibits a high sensitivity with a low detection limit for determination of estradiol [22]. However, Pd nanoparticles in Pd-graphene composite can easily fall off from graphene sheets during heating and reduction because of the lack of strong interaction between graphene and Pd nanoparticles. More importantly, hybrid of Pd and graphene cannot break through the limitations of their inherent properties and expand new applications because Pd and graphene are conductors.

In the study, we reported one strategy for synthesis of palladium-graphene composite using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) as linker and semiconductor. The resuting Pd-His-GQD-G shows one three-dimensional architecture with excellent dispersion and Schottky heterojunction. Unique structure achieves to an improved catalytic activity towards oxidation of 4-nitrophenol. Thermal-switchable sensor based on the Pd-His-GQD-G and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) provides the advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and long-term stability for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol.

Histidine, citric acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), palladium chloride (PdCl2), poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC), 2-chlorophenol (2-CP), nitrobenzene (NB), 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4-KCl, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) and [Fe(CN)6]4- (1 mM) was prepared. Graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from natural graphite by Hummers’ method [23]. His-GQD was synthesized by using one reported method [24].

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were conducted on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were conducted on JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm). Raman measurements were carried out using InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on PHI 5700 ESCA electron spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu).

GO (0.5 g) was mixed with 150 mL of doubly distilled water, treated for 1 h by ultrasound to form homogeneous dispersion, dropped 50 mL of His-GQD aqueous solution (14 mg mL−1) and treated for 0.5 h by ultrasound in sequence. Followed by adjusting the acidity to pH 7 with 1 M NaOH aqueous solution, dropping 7 mL of the PdCl2 aqueous solution (0.05 M) under stirring to form Pd/His-GQD/GO complex, centrifugation at 5000 r/min and washing with doubly distilled water for three times. The collected solid sample was dried in a vacuum at 50℃ and thermally reduced at 400 ℃ for 4 h in a N2 atmosphere to obtain Pd-His-GQD-G.

Firstly, 10 mg of Pd-His-GQD-G was dispersed in 5 mL of doubly distilled water under ultrasound to form homogeneous dispersion, and subsequently mixed with 5 mL of PNIPAM (10 mg mL−1) under ultrasound to form Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM composite. Then, its 5 μL was dropped on the surface of pre-treated GCE to obtain electrochemical sensor. The as-prepared sensor was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C before use.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out on CHI660D electrochemical workstation, Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode and platinum wire as a counter electrode. All experiments were measured in the PBS of pH 7 containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was measured between −1.2 and 0.6 V with the sweep speed was 100 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were measured at the frequency range 100 KHz and 1 Hz with the potential amplitude of ± 5 mV. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) parameters were set to between −0.3 and −0.9 V.

Synthesis of Pd-His-GQD-G involves three steps (shown in Fig. 1
A). First, His-GQD is mixed with GO in an aqueous medium to form stable His-GQD/GO composite. As His-GQD has a lot of hydrophilic groups that can bond to GO via hydrogen bonds and van der Waals force, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and imidazole groups, the combination of His-GQDs with GO improves the hydrophilicity. Thus, the above mixing will achieve to the immobilization of His-GQD on the surface of graphene sheets from GO via the π-π stacking. Then, the PdCl2 aqueous solution was dropped into the above system with stirring. Because the imidazole groups in His-GQD can coordinate with Pd2+ ions to form stable complex, the addition of PdCl2 realizes the immobilization of Pd2+ ions on the surface of large graphene sheet from GO. This helps to improve the dispersion of Pd-His-GQD-G and reduce the size of Pd nanoparticles. As one Pd2+ ion can simultaneously coordinate with four imidazole groups at different His-GQDs, the above coordination also causes a rapid polymerization process of graphene sheets and finally leads to the formation of three-dimensional structure. To obtain Pd-His-GQD-G, the formed complex was thermally reduced at 400 ℃ for 4 h in a N2 environment. During the heating, the most of active groups on His-GQD and GO were decomposed. This will greatly improve the electrical conductivity. At the same time, Pd2+ is reduced to Pd0, finally leading to formation of Pd nanocrystals. Due to strong coordination between Pd2+ with imidazole group, Pd2+ ions were slowly released from the complex during the reduction. This is conducive to form small size of Pd nanoparticles.

The as-synthesized Pd-His-GQD-G was characterized by SEM, TEM, High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and XRD and Raman spectrum. Fig. 1B-G show that Pd-His-GQD-G offers one three-dimensional structure. Small Pd nanoparticles of about 18 nm were uniformly dispersed on the graphene sheets. There is one crystal spacing of 0.23 nm on the HRTEM image, corresponding to the (111) plane of Pd crystal. The EDS spectrum shows that Pd-His-GQD-G contains C, N, O and Pd elements (shown in Fig. s1). There are five distinct diffraction peaks at 26°, 40.1°, 46.7°, 68.1° and 82.1° on the XRD pattern. The diffraction peak at 26° can be assigned to the (002) plane of graphene. The diffraction peaks at 40.1°, 46.7°, 68.1° and 82.1° are corresponded to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of Pd (JCPDS No. 46–1043), respectively. There are two characteristic Raman peaks of carbon materials on the Raman spectrum. The two peaks are orderly corresponded to the G band and D band of graphene material [25].

Fig. s2 presents the XPS spectra of Pd-His-GQD-G. Total XPS spectrum shows Pd-His-GQD-G is composed of C, N, O and Pd elements. High resolution C1s XPS spectrum can be devided into four peaks at 283.9 eV, 285.35 eV, 287.2 eV and 288.7 eV. These peaks are corresponded to the C–C, C-O, C = O and O-C = O, respectively. High resolution N1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 398.3, 399.9 and 401 eV, corresponding to the graphitic N, amide N and pyridinic N. High resolution Pd3d spectrum can be divided into four peaks, belonging to the Pd0 (335.4 and 340.4 eV) and Pd2+ (337.3 and 342.5 eV). The result demonstrates the existence of Pd0 and Pd2+ on the surface of Pd nanoparticles.

Mott-Schottky plots of His-GQD, His-GQD-G and Pd-His-GQD-G electrodes were measured in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution at the frequency of 1000 Hz to confirm the formation of Schottky heterojunction. Fig. s3 shows three line sections of the plots have a positive slope, which proves that His-GQD, His-GQD-G and Pd-His-GQD-G are n-type semiconductors. The flat band potential (Vb) can be obtained by extrapolating the Mott-Schottky plots. The Vb of His-GQD, His-GQD-G and Pd-His-GQD-G are −0.14 V, −0.36 V and −0.44 V, respectively. The above date show that the combination of His-GQD, graphene and Pd results in negative shift of Vb. The Fermi level of His-GQD should shift a corresponding absolute value of Vb towards the conduction band edge after the combination with graphene and Pd, the degree of up-shift for His-GQD-G and Pd-His-GQD-G increases. The up-shift of Fermi level brings an increased electron concentration and improved electrical conductivity.

To evaluate electrochemical properties of Pd-His-GQD-G, the electrochemical behavior of proposed sensor with different materials was investigated in the PBS of pH 7 containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4- by the CV and EIS measurements. Fig. 2 indicates that bare GCE gives one pair of redox peaks on the CV curve. This is attributed to the redox of [Fe(CN)6]4- on the electrode surface. The CV current will obviously reduce with increasing the charge transfer impedance (Rct) when PNIPAM was immobilized on the electrode surface. This is due to the poor electrical conductivity of PNIPAM. However, the introduction of Pd-His-GQD-G causes an obvious increase in the CV current with a reduced Rct value. The result demonstrates the Pd-His-GQD-G has a high catalytic activity towards the redox of [Fe(CN)6]4-. We suggest such a high catalytic activity is attributed to high dispersion and three-dimensional structure and Schottky heterojunction of Pd-His-GQD-G. These factors accelerate the electrode reaction and leads to an increased CV current.

The electrochemical behavior of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM electrode at different temperatures was investigated in a PBS of pH 7 containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4- by EIS technoque. Fig. 3 shows the Rct value slowly decreases with the increase of temperature when the temperature is less than 28℃. The Rct suddenly decreases greatly with the increase of temperature when the temperature is between 28 and 32 ℃. This is because PNIPAM is a temperature-sensitive polymer [26]. When the temperature is lower than the lower critical solution temperature (LCST, 32℃), PNIPAM shows high hydrophilicity, which can encapsulate the Pd-His-GQD-G inside, resulting in the blocking of the active site, and preventing the diffusion of the probe to the electrode surface for electron exchange. However, PNIPAM transforms into a hydrophobic contraction state when the temperature is higher than LCST. This brings more exposure of active sites on the surface of Pd nanoparticles. This will greatly improve the catalytic activity and results in a reduced Rct value. The above results also demonstrate that Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM has a good thermal-switchable behaviour.

The reaction kinetics of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE was studied in a PBS of pH 7.0 containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4- at 36 ℃. Fig. s4 shows the CV curves of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM at different scanning rates in the range of 20–200 mV s−1. It can be seen from the results that the peak current increases with the increase of scanning rates. And the peak current of the cathode and anode is linearly proportional with the square root of scan rates. The regression equations were Ipa (μA)= (2.99 ± 0.084) ν1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 + (9.46 ± 0.82) (R2 = 0.992), Ipc (μA)= (-3.48 ± 0.11) ν1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 + (-11.94 ± 1.0) (R2 = 0.990). In addition, an increase in the scan rate caused a positive shift in the oxidation peak potentials and a negative shift in the reduction peak potentials. This indicates that the electrode process at the Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM modified electrode surface is diffusional and quasi-reversible.

The electrochemical response of proposed sensor towards 4-NP was studied by CV and DPV measurements in the PBS of pH 7 in the absence and the presence of 0.125 mM 4-NP at 36 ℃. Fig. s5 indicates no redox peak can be observed from the CV curve in the absence of 4-NP. This demonstrated that Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM can not be oxidized and reduced in the potential range from −1.2 V to 0.6 V. However, in the presence of 4-NP several pairs of redox peaks appear on the CV curve. This verifies that Pd-His-GQD-G can catalyze the redox of 4-NP on the electrode surface due to its high catalytic activity. For the electrode reaction, a possible mechanism was suggested at Fig. s6. The reduction peak at −0.767 V is produced by the reduction of 4-NP into 4-hydroxyaminophenol via four-electron and four-proton transfer step. With the potential sweeping back, one pair of reversible redox conversion occurs between 4-hydroxyaminophenol and 4-nitrophenol, and an oxidation peak and a reduction peak appear at the potentials of 0.239 V and 0.097 V, respectively [27]. Further, Fig. s5B shows the DPV curves in the absence and presnece of 4-NP. It can be seen from Fig. s5 that the DPV current response towards 4-NP is much higher than that of the CV peak current. To obtain high sensitivity, the DPV technique was selected for electrochemical detection of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on 4-NP electrochemical behaviors at Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM modified electrode was studied by DPV in 0.1 M PBS of 7.0 containing 0.125 mM 4-NP at different temperatures from 16 to 38℃. As shown in Fig. s7 (A-B), the solution temperature increased from 16 ℃ to 24 ℃, the DPV response peak current change is small. However, when the solution temperature exceeds 24 ℃, the DPV peak current enhanced significantly. These results suggest that the Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM has excellent temperature responsive behavior. The mechanism is shown in Fig. 4 . When the temperature of the solution is below 36 ℃, the stretch of the PNIPAM chains causes the Pd-His-GQD-G to be wrapped inside, which prevents the diffusion of 4-NP to the electrode surface for electron exchange. It shows that the electrode is “OFF” state. When the solution temperature is above 36 ℃, the contraction of PNIPAM brings a large number of active sites of Pd-His-GQD-G exposed, which greatly improves the 4-NP electron transfer rate. It shows that the modified electrode is in the “ON” state. Therefore, the “ON/OFF” switch of the modified electrode can be used for the determination of 4-NP by changing the temperature of the solution. Further, the temperature reversibility and stability Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM modified electrode for the determination of 4-NP was investigated. Fig. s7 (C-D) showed the DPV curves of Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE were obtained by repeated the heating and cooling of the 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.0 containing 0.125 mM 4-NP at 18℃ and 36℃. When the Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM modified electrode is heated and cooled for 6 times, the peak current has no obvious change, indicating that Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM/GCE has excellent temperature reversibility and stability.

To optimize the detection conditions, the influence of PBS of different pH on the electrochemical detection of 4-NP was studied. Fig. s8 shows that the cathodic peak current will gradually increase with the increase of pH value when the pH value increases from 3 to 7. However, the cathodic peak current will decrease when the pH is more than 7. Therefore, the pH of 7 was selected for electrochemical detection of 4-NP. Further, Fig. s8B indicates the relationship curve between different pH value and the cathode peak potentials. When the pH value increases in range of 3–9, the cathode peak potential shifts to more negative. The linear regression equation is Epc = (-0.0546 ± 0.0015) [pH] + (-0.2886 ± 0.0097), R2 = 0.996. The slope in the equation is −54.6 mV pH−1, which is close to the theory value of −59.2 mV pH−1. These results indicated that the number of protons and electrons involved in the reduction reaction in the electrocatalytic process of 4-NP are the same.

Ten Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM electrodes were prepared by using the same procedure and their DPV curves were measured in 0.1 M PBS of 7.0 containing 0.125 mM of 4-NP at 36 ℃. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of their peak current was found to be 2.12%, indicating a good reproducibility. The prepared sensor was tested for detection of 0.125 mM 4-NP for 20 consecutive detections. The RSD of these detections was 1.92%, verifying good repeatability. The Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM sensor and Pd-His-GQD-G sensor were placed in the refrigerator at 4 ℃. After four weeks, the peak current was still 98.5% and 96.1% of the initial value. The above data shows that the Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM electrode exhibits a better stability in the DPV response compared with Pd-His-GQD-G electrode. This demonstrates that the introduction of PNIPAM brings an improved long-term stability. To further confirm the stability of catalytic activity, Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM and Pd-His-GQD-G were placed in the refrigerator at 4 ℃ for four weeks. Then, these were taken out and separately used to catalyze the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue product every week. Fig. s9 shows after four weeks Pd-His-GQD-G/PNIPAM maintained a much higher catalytic activity than Pd-His-GQD-G. This is because the PNIPAM prevents highly active sites on the surface of Pd nanoparticles from reacting with the surrounding medium including air and water.

The selectivity of the sensor is further studied. The effect of the coexisting inorganic ions (SO4 2-, Br-, Na+, Ca2+) and structurally similar compounds (HQ, CC, 2-CP, NB and DNP) on the detection of 4-NP was investigated. The results show that the addition of 100-fold the concentration of inorganic ions hardly changes the peak current response. The addition of 1 µM HQ, CC and 4-CP causes the reduction peak current change to be less than 5 % (shown in Fig. s10). The addition of 1 µM NB causes the reduction peak current to change by about 10 %. The addition of 1 µM DNP causes the reduction peak current to change by about 17 %. This is due to the presence of the same reducing group (nitro group), resulting in a reduction current at the 4-NP reduction potential.

The proposed sensor has been used for detection of 4-NP in tap water, lake water and sea water. All water samples were collected in Jiangsu Province. The collected samples were firstly filtered using 0.45 μm syringe filters and then their pH values were adjusted to pH 7.0. The 4-NP concentration was measured by the proposed sensor. In addition, the addition of a known concentration of 4-NP into the water sample was used for investigating the recovery of the proposed method. Table 2 shows the recovery is between 97 and 100%, indicating a good precision and accuracy.

This study has demonstrated synthesis of palladium-histidine functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene (Pd-His-GQD-G). The introduction of His-GQD achieves to good dispersion of Pd nanoparticles on graphene sheet and formation of Schottky heterojunction. Unique structure leads to a high catalytic activity of Pd-His-GQD-G. Thermal-switchable sensor based on Pd-His-GQD-G and poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) exhibits high sensitivity, selectivity and long-term stability for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol.
Xu Dan: Investigation. Li Ruiyi: Investigation. Wang Qinsheng: Investigation. Yang Yongqiang: Investigation. Wang Guangli: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603001), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20-1776), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute (KJ(Y)2020037) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106970.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal organic frameworks as robust host of palladium nanoparticles in heterogeneous catalysis: synthesis, application, and prospect
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Luo, Z.T. Zeng, G.M. Zeng, Z.F. Liu, R. Xiao, M. Chen, L. Tang, W.W. Tang, C. Lai, M. Cheng, B.B. Shao, Q.H. Liang, H. Wang, D.N. Jiang, Metal organic frameworks as robust host of palladium nanoparticles in heterogeneous catalysis: synthesis, application, and prospect, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 11 (2019) 32579-32598. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.9b11990.
